turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Alito has the opinion this morning in case 16-254, Water Splash versus Menon, which he has asked me to announce. This case concerns the scope of the Hague Service Convention, a multilateral treaty which was designed to improve the process of serving documents abroad. The question is whether the Convention prohibits service by mail, and we hold that it does not. Petitioner, Water Splash brought a lawsuit against respondent, Menon in Texas State Court. Menon is a resident of Canada, so the Trial Court allowed Water Splash to affect the service of process by mail. When Menon failed to answer, the Trial Court entered a default judgment in favor of Water Splash, but a Texas Court of Appeals reversed the judgment holding that the Hague Service Convention does not allow service by mail. We granted certiorari. The key provision for our purposes is Article 10(a) of the Convention, which states that as long as the receiving state does not object, the Convention shall not interfere with the freedom to send judicial documents by postal channels directly to persons abroad. Other courts have disagreed as to whether Article 10(a) encompasses service by mail. We conclude that it does. In interpreting treaties, we begin with the words of the treaty and the context in which those words are used. Here are the broad term “send” is most naturally read to include sending documents for the purpose of service and the relevant context strongly reinforces that reading as we have previously held the Convention applies only to documents transmitted for service abroad. Article 1 in particular eliminates the possibility that the Convention would apply to any communications that do not culminate in service. This supports Water Splash's view that Article 10(a) encompasses service by mail, otherwise, Article 10(a) would be both anomalous as the only provision of the Convention that did not apply to service of documents, and superfluous because it would have no work to do. In short, the text and structure of the Convention strongly suggest that Article 10(a) pertains to service of documents. But even assuming that the Convention is ambiguous on this point, other traditional tools of treaty interpretation would resolve that ambiguity in favor of Water Splash. In particular, the Convention's drafting history; the long standing position of the executive branch and the views of other signatories all indicate that the Convention does not prohibit service by mail. For these and other reasons described in our opinion, we vacate the judgment of the Court of Appeals and we remand the case for further proceedings, not inconsistent with this opinion. Our decision is unanimous. Justice Gorsuch took no part in the consideration or decision of this case.
